title: UAE Reports Three Wounded After Iranian Missile and Drone Barrage on May 9
summary: The United Arab Emirates Defense Ministry confirmed that Iranian forces launched two ballistic missiles and three drones targeting UAE territory, wounding three people. UAE air defenses engaged the incoming projectiles amid ongoing Gulf tensions.
contents:
## UAE Targeted by Iranian Missile and Drone Strike

### Incident Overview

The United Arab Emirates Defense Ministry announced on May 9, 2026, that Iranian forces launched a combined attack consisting of two ballistic missiles and three unmanned aerial vehicles targeting Emirati territory. The attack wounded three people with moderate injuries.

### Defense Response

UAE air defense systems engaged the incoming projectiles. Authorities instructed civilians to avoid areas where debris had fallen. The UAE has deployed Israeli and American-supplied defense systems, including advanced interceptor platforms, to counter the ongoing Iranian aerial threat.

### Regional Context

This attack represents the latest in a series of Iranian strikes against the UAE throughout the ongoing US-Iran conflict. Iranian Parliament member Ali Khezrian stated on May 8 that Iran now considers the UAE a "hostile base" rather than a neighbor. Supreme Leader Advisor Mohammad Mokhber declared the UAE "has received punishment" and warned of further consequences.

### Diplomatic Implications

The strike occurred as Washington awaited Tehran's response to a peace proposal. The UAE has demanded that any ceasefire agreement include limitations on Iran's missile and drone capabilities, going beyond the current framework focused solely on reopening the Strait of Hormuz.

title: Indian Cargo Vessel Capsizes in Strait of Hormuz After Fire, One Sailor Killed
summary: An Indian wooden dhow carrying 18 crew members caught fire and capsized near the Strait of Hormuz. One Gujarat sailor died, four others sustained severe burns, and 17 survivors received rescue from a passing vessel near Dubai.
contents:
## Indian Cargo Vessel Capsizes Near Strait of Hormuz

### Incident Details

The Indian cargo vessel Al Faize Noore Sulemani 1, carrying 18 crew members, caught fire and subsequently capsized near the Strait of Hormuz on May 9, 2026. The vessel had departed Dubai on May 7, heading toward Mukalla in Yemen, when the incident occurred while crossing the strategic waterway.

### Casualties and Rescue

One Gujarat sailor perished in the incident, and four other crew members sustained severe burns. A passing vessel rescued the remaining 17 sailors, who received transport to Dubai Port for medical treatment. The Indian Consulate in Dubai has coordinated assistance for the rescued crew and arranged the return of the deceased sailor's remains.

### Regional Tensions Factor

The incident occurred amid escalating tensions between Iran and the United States in the Strait of Hormuz. Reports indicate the vessel may have come under fire while transiting the waterway. The strait has remained effectively closed to most commercial shipping since the US-Iran conflict intensified, with no observed outbound or inbound transits recorded since earlier in the week.

### Broader Maritime Impact

This casualty adds to the growing toll of maritime incidents in the waterway. The International Maritime Organization has documented 32 vessel attacks since the conflict began, stranding hundreds of ships and approximately 20,000 seafarers inside the Gulf.

title: Australia and Japan Enhance Defense Cooperation with New Strategic Depth Agreement
summary: Australian Prime Minister Albanese and Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi finalized enhanced defense cooperation commitments in Canberra, introducing strategic depth to the bilateral military partnership with mutual weapons testing, co-production and supply chain security.
contents:
## Australia-Japan Enhanced Defense and Security Cooperation

### Agreement Overview

Australia and Japan signed a joint statement on Enhanced Defence and Security Cooperation on May 4, 2026, during Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's visit to Canberra. The agreement builds on the 2022 Joint Declaration, the 2023 Reciprocal Access Agreement, and the 2025 Framework for Strategic Defence Cooperation.

### Key Priorities

The enhanced cooperation framework covers several critical areas:
- Increased intelligence sharing between defense establishments
- Co-development, co-production and co-sustainment of defense capabilities
- Advanced weapons testing on Australian territory
- Enhanced joint training and military exercises
- Joint maintenance and sustainment of defense assets
- Closer collaboration on supply chain security and critical maritime routes

### Strategic Depth Concept

Analysts at the Australian Strategic Policy Institute characterize the agreement as introducing "strategic depth" to the partnership. Japan's limited geography makes it vulnerable to first-strike scenarios, while Australia offers vast territory for force dispersal, joint testing, and secure logistics. The agreement alludes to potential Japanese force deployments to Australia during a crisis.

### Indo-Pacific Implications

The cooperation specifically targets securing critical maritime traffic routes between the Indian Ocean and northeast Asia. Both nations also pledged cyber defense cooperation and mineral supply chain collaboration, recognizing these domains as fundamental to military readiness in the current strategic environment.

title: Germany Raises Security Level in Libyan SAR Zone After Coast Guard Attacks on Rescue Vessels
summary: The German Ministry of the Interior raised the maritime security level to Level 2 in the Libyan search and rescue zone for the first time, citing violent attacks by the Libyan coast guard against civilian rescue ships. Sea-Watch and SOS Humanity called for an end to EU-funded Libyan coast guard training.
contents:
## Germany Elevates Security Classification for Libyan Waters

### Security Level Change

On May 7, 2026, the German Ministry of the Interior extended Security Level 2 classification from Libyan territorial waters to encompass the entire Libyan search and rescue zone off the Libyan coast. This marks the first time since civilian search and rescue operations began in the central Mediterranean in 2015 that Germany has applied this heightened security designation to the operational area used by organizations such as Sea-Watch and SOS Humanity.

### Trigger and Context

The German Maritime Security Centre communicated the change to NGOs whose rescue vessels fly the German flag. The Ministry specifically assessed that civilian rescue ships face particular risk alongside commercial vessels. The escalation followed years of documented violence by the so-called Libyan coast guard, including the August 2025 shooting at the civilian rescue ship Ocean Viking.

### NGO Response

Marie Naass, Head of Political Communications at Sea-Watch, highlighted a fundamental contradiction in German policy: acknowledging the danger posed by the Libyan coast guard while continuing to support and train that same force. SOS Humanity policy expert Marie Michel criticized the delayed recognition, noting years of documented violence and human rights violations.

### Mediterranean Death Toll

The security reclassification comes amid alarming casualty figures. The International Organization for Migration reports at least 990 deaths in Mediterranean crossings during 2026, making it the deadliest start to a year since 2014. In the central Mediterranean alone, approximately 765 people have died, representing over 460 more fatalities than the same period last year.

title: China MND Confirms Carrier Air Wing Complete with Five Aircraft Types
summary: China's Ministry of National Defense spokesperson confirmed the PLA Navy has completed its carrier air wing suite with stealth fighters, multi-role aircraft, airborne early warning, electronic warfare planes, and anti-submarine helicopters, marking a major naval modernization milestone.
contents:
## PLA Navy Completes Carrier Air Wing Configuration

### Official Announcement

On May 9, 2026, Ministry of National Defense spokesperson Senior Colonel Jiang Bin confirmed during a regular press briefing that the PLA Navy has successfully assembled the complete suite of five carrier-based aircraft types. Chinese media has dubbed this achievement the "carrier five-piece set."

### Aircraft Composition

The completed carrier air wing now includes:
- Stealth carrier-based fighter aircraft
- Multi-role catapult-launched carrier-based fighter
- Fixed-wing carrier-based airborne early warning aircraft
- Fixed-wing electronic warfare aircraft
- Anti-submarine helicopter

### Strategic Significance

Jiang described this capability as "an important component in forming the carrier battle group's systematic combat capability" and "an important achievement of the PLA Navy's accelerated transformation." The announcement signals that China's carrier strike groups now possess the full spectrum of airborne capabilities needed for integrated operations.

### Additional Announcements

The same press briefing also revealed that China and Thailand will conduct the "Assault-2026" joint army training exercise in Thailand in mid-to-late May, focusing on mountain jungle counter-terrorism operations. Jiang also confirmed the delivery of the first Hangor-class submarine to Pakistan, calling it a reflection of the all-weather strategic partnership between the two nations.

title: US Navy Disables Two Iranian Tankers Attempting to Breach Blockade in Gulf of Oman
summary: US Central Command reported a fighter jet struck the smokestacks of two Iranian-flagged tankers, Sea Star III and Sevda, after they attempted to enter an Iranian port in the Gulf of Oman. US forces now prevent more than 70 tankers from accessing Iranian ports.
contents:
## US Strikes Iranian Tankers Breaching Naval Blockade

### Incident Details

On May 9, 2026, US Central Command announced that American naval forces disabled two Iranian tankers attempting to breach the US blockade of Iranian ports. A US Navy fighter jet fired into the smokestacks of the vessels Sea Star III and Sevda after they attempted to enter an Iranian port along the Gulf of Oman.

### Blockade Scope

CENTCOM stated that US naval forces have redirected 57 vessels since the blockade commenced on April 13. American forces currently prevent more than 70 tankers from entering or departing Iranian ports as part of the ongoing maritime blockade. These vessels collectively hold capacity for more than 166 million barrels of Iranian oil, valued at approximately $13 billion.

### Iran's Persian Gulf Strait Authority

Iran has established a new agency called the Persian Gulf Strait Authority, positioning itself as the sole valid authority to grant permission for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio called this development "unacceptable," challenging whether the world would accept Iranian control over an international waterway.

### Market Impact

Brent crude futures continued hovering around $100 per barrel as traders weighed conflicting signals from Gulf clashes against reports of diplomatic progress. The International Energy Agency has characterized the situation as "the biggest energy security threat in history," with approximately 13 million barrels of oil per day removed from the market.

title: US Sanctions Chinese Entities Providing Satellite Imagery to Iran for Military Strikes
summary: The US State Department imposed sanctions on 11 entities and three individuals across Iran, China, Belarus, and the UAE for supporting Iran's war efforts, including Chinese companies providing satellite imagery enabling Iranian military strikes against US forces.
contents:
## US Sanctions Target Chinese Satellite Imagery Providers to Iran

### Sanctions Announcement

The US State Department announced on May 9, 2026, new sanctions targeting 11 entities and three individuals based in Iran, China, Belarus, and the United Arab Emirates for allegedly supporting Iran's war efforts. Secretary of State Marco Rubio highlighted Chinese entities as a particular concern.

### Technology Transfer Focus

The sanctions specifically target several China-based entities providing satellite imagery that enables Iran's military strikes against US forces in the Middle East. Additional designations cover entities and individuals facilitating weapons procurement and raw materials acquisition for Iran's ballistic missile and unmanned aerial vehicle programs.

### Intelligence Implications

The designation of satellite imagery providers reveals a previously underappreciated dimension of the Iran conflict: the role of commercial satellite intelligence in enabling Iranian targeting of US naval assets. This technology transfer enables precision strikes that Iran's indigenous reconnaissance capabilities alone could not support.

### Russian Drone Offer to Iran

Separately, The Economist reported having reviewed confidential Russian documents revealing a Russian proposal to supply Iran with several thousand drones and training for Iranian drone operators. This raises concerns about the proliferation of fiber-optic drone technology to Iran and its regional proxies, some of which have already demonstrated the ability to employ these systems against US and allied targets.

### Broader Sanctions Context

The latest sanctions complement the ongoing US naval blockade, which has redirected 57 vessels and currently prevents more than 70 tankers from accessing Iranian ports. The combined economic pressure aims to force Tehran toward accepting US terms for ending the conflict.

title: Iran Keeps US Waiting on Peace Proposal Response as Strait of Hormuz Clashes Continue
summary: Iran questioned US diplomatic seriousness after renewed naval clashes in the Gulf, while keeping Washington waiting for a response to the latest peace proposal. Foreign Minister Araghchi accused the US of violating the ceasefire through military action against Iranian tankers.
contents:
## Iran Delays Response to US Peace Proposal Amid Gulf Clashes

### Diplomatic Standoff

On May 9, 2026, Iran continued to delay its response to the US peace proposal, with Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi questioning American diplomatic credibility during a call with his Turkish counterpart. Araghchi stated that "the recent escalation of tensions by American forces in the Persian Gulf and their numerous actions in violating the ceasefire have added to suspicions about the motivation and seriousness of the American side."

### US Proposal Framework

The US proposal, transmitted via Pakistani mediators, seeks to formally end the conflict first before addressing core demands including suspension of Iran's nuclear program and reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baqaei confirmed the proposal remained "under review."

### IRGC Dominance in Decision-Making

US intelligence assessments indicate that Iran's new supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who sustained serious injuries during the war's opening strikes, communicates only through in-person meetings and couriers. Senior IRGC officials, particularly Commander Ahmad Vahidi, and Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf effectively run day-to-day operations. This fractured leadership structure has complicated negotiations.

### Ceasefire Under Strain

Despite President Trump's insistence that the month-old ceasefire holds, the US struck Iranian military facilities in the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday after reporting attacks on three Navy destroyers. Iran accused the US of targeting an oil tanker and civilian areas on Qeshm Island. One Iranian sailor reportedly died and 10 suffered injuries in the overnight exchanges.

title: US Army Stages Military Equipment at Subic Bay for Balikatan Drills and Alliance Contingencies
summary: The US Army utilized the American-owned Agila Subic facility to stage military equipment for Balikatan 2026 exercises and alliance contingencies, with security contractor Continuity Global Solutions providing armed protection for sensitive military assets stored at the strategic bay.
contents:
## US Military Expands Logistics Footprint at Subic Bay

### Equipment Staging Operations

The US Army utilized Agila Subic, a 300-hectare complex owned by American investment firm Cerberus Capital Management, to stage military equipment during the Balikatan 2026 exercise cycle. Army trucks and helicopters rolled off a Military Sealift Command-contracted vessel at Subic Bay, with equipment stored at the facility rather than the civilian port used in previous years.

### Security Arrangements

The Army contracted American security firm Continuity Global Solutions to provide armed protection for two sites in Subic Bay, protecting sensitive military assets staged in the Philippines between 2026 and 2027. Contract documents indicate the Army expects a more sustained logistics operation, with potential transition of responsibility from the 8th Theater Sustainment Command to the Army's sustainment command.

### Balikatan 2026 Context

The largest-ever Balikatan exercises concluded on May 8, involving over 17,000 troops from seven nations. Key milestones included Japan firing Type 88 anti-ship missiles on Philippine territory for the first time and the US Army launching a Tomahawk cruise missile from the Typhon system at Tacloban City. Australia contributed forces to cross-decking exercises with ships from the US, Canada, and Japan.

### Chinese Espionage Concerns

Subic Bay has faced alleged Chinese espionage operations, with Philippine authorities apprehending several Chinese nationals suspected of cataloging US and Philippine warships at the facility. The arrests occurred during a broader nationwide crackdown on alleged Chinese monitoring activities at strategic military and political sites.

title: China MND Condemns Japan-Philippines Military Cooperation, Warns Against Alliance Building
summary: China's Ministry of National Defense spokesperson strongly opposed Japan-Philippines joint defense statements, condemning Japan for dispatching combat troops to the Philippines and firing offensive missiles overseas for the first time, while warning both countries to stop building military alliances targeting China.
contents:
## China Denounces Japan-Philippines Military Collusion

### MND Statement

During the May 9, 2026 press briefing, Chinese MND spokesperson Senior Colonel Jiang Bin issued a sharp rebuke of recent Japan-Philippines defense cooperation. Jiang stated that China "strongly opposes" what he characterized as false narratives regarding maritime issues and groundless smearing of China by Japanese and Philippine officials.

### Specific Grievances

Jiang highlighted several provocative developments:
- Japan dispatching combat troops to participate in the Balikatan exercises, marking the first overseas deployment of Japanese ground forces in a combat-capable role since World War II
- Japan firing Type 88 anti-ship missiles on Philippine territory, characterized as deliberately breaching the "exclusively defense-oriented" principle
- The Philippines attempting to recruit external powers to support its maritime claims in the South China Sea

### Taiwan Strait Tensions

In response to questions about PLA activities near Taiwan, Jiang declared that "Taiwan belongs to China" and no "median line" of the Taiwan Strait exists. He confirmed the PLA conducted combat readiness patrols around Taiwan and deployed warships into waters southwest of the Penghu Islands, linking the activities to recent high-level visits by Taiwanese officials.

### Strategic Warning

Jiang urged relevant countries to "stop forming cliques for bloc confrontation" and instead pursue actions genuinely beneficial to regional peace and stability. The statement reflects growing Chinese concern about the expanding trilateral US-Japan-Philippines security framework linking the East China Sea, Taiwan Strait, and South China Sea flashpoints.

links.label: Sina Finance - MND Briefing Full Text

title: Japan Defense Acquisition Agency Orders 300 Domestic Drones from Terra Drone
summary: Japan's Acquisition, Technology and Logistics Agency (ATLA) has contracted Terra Drone for 300 modular UAVs worth 115 million yen, marking a significant step in domestic drone procurement for defense purposes. Delivery remains scheduled for September 30, 2026.
contents:
## Japan Advances Domestic Drone Procurement for Defense

### Overview
Japan's defense acquisition arm, ATLA, finalized a 115.4 million yen ($780,000) manufacturing contract with Tokyo-based Terra Drone for 300 modular UAVs designated as educational platforms for the Japan Self-Defense Forces. The announcement, released on May 8, represents a concrete step in Japan's national policy of positioning unmanned systems at the core of its defense architecture.

### Strategic Context
Terra Drone entered the defense sector in March 2026, subsequently investing in Ukrainian counter-drone company Amazing Drones and deploying operational drones in Ukraine by April. The company characterized the contract as validation of Japan's domestic drone supply capability amid a global security environment undergoing fundamental transformation.

### Defense Industrial Implications
The procurement aligns with recommendations from ATLA's February report on strengthening Japan's defense industrial and technological base, which emphasized encouraging startups into defense through flexible contracts and maximizing commercial component usage in military drones. Prime Minister Takaichi's advisory panel on national security has specifically highlighted drone warfare lessons from Ukraine as a priority for Japan's revised defense strategy.

### Delivery and Next Steps
All 300 units carry a September 30 delivery deadline. The contract signals accelerating momentum in Japan's push to develop indigenous unmanned systems capability, moving beyond reliance on imported platforms.

title: Strait of Hormuz Shipping at Complete Standstill for 48 Hours Amid US-Iran Clashes
summary: No major commercial vessels have transited the Strait of Hormuz for 48 consecutive hours as US and Iranian naval forces exchange fire, with Iran attacking three US Navy destroyers and the US disabling Iranian-flagged tankers attempting to breach the blockade.
contents:
## Hormuz Shipping Crisis Deepens as Naval Clashes Escalate

### Current Situation
Ship tracking data compiled by Anadolu Agency confirms zero major commercial vessel transits through the Strait of Hormuz for a consecutive 48-hour period as of 0900 GMT on May 9. The standstill follows renewed exchanges of fire between US and Iranian forces in and around the critical waterway.

### Naval Engagements
Iranian forces launched missiles, drones, and fast attack craft against USS Mason (DDG-87), USS Rafael Peralta (DDG-115), and USS Truxton (DDG-103) as the destroyers transited toward the Gulf of Oman. US Central Command characterized the attacks as unprovoked and confirmed defensive responses. President Trump stated no damage occurred to the three destroyers while characterizing significant damage to Iranian attackers.

### Tanker Operations
US F/A-18 Super Hornets from USS George H.W. Bush (CVN-77) fired on two Iranian-flagged tankers, MT Sea Star III and MT Sevda, disabling them in the Gulf of Oman. Earlier in the week, another Super Hornet disabled tanker MT Hasna by firing 20mm cannon rounds into her rudder. Three cargo-empty tankers belonging to National Iranian Tanker Company managed to cross through the US Navy blockade line via Pakistan's Exclusive Economic Zone.

### Iran's Persian Gulf Strait Authority
Iran has formalized control over Hormuz transits through a newly created Persian Gulf Strait Authority, requiring all vessels to submit detailed declarations including cargo, crew nationalities, and destinations before receiving transit permission. The authority reportedly charges up to $2 million per vessel.

### Market Impact
US gasoline prices have risen above $4.50 per gallon. Lloyd's List tracked only 40 transits in the entire week ending May 3, compared to a pre-war average of 120 crossings per day.

title: Philippine Coast Guard Detects 40+ Chinese Vessels Including Research Ship Near Spratly Islands
summary: The Philippine Coast Guard spotted more than 40 Chinese vessels, including oceanographic research vessel Xiang Yang Hong 33 and Chinese maritime militia ships, conducting unauthorized activities near the Spratly Islands within the Philippine exclusive economic zone.
contents:
## Philippines Challenges Chinese Maritime Presence in Spratly Waters

### Detection Summary
Philippine Coast Guard aircraft Islander 4177 detected Chinese oceanographic research vessel Xiang Yang Hong 33 anchored approximately 7.34 nautical miles west of Rozul Reef during a maritime domain awareness flight. The vessel deployed a service boat heading toward the reef, confirming ongoing unauthorized marine scientific research operations.

### Vessel Disposition
The PCG identified Chinese Coast Guard vessel BN 5309 positioned at the western part of Rozul Reef alongside 13 Chinese maritime militia vessels anchored in surrounding waters. Near Pag-asa (Thitu) Island, an additional 28 Chinese maritime militia vessels clustered around Cay 1 and Cay 2, while China Coast Guard vessel 5101 maintained position approximately two nautical miles west of the island.

### Research Vessel Tracking
Canada's dark vessel detection system tracked Xiang Yang Hong 33 conducting survey activities across multiple Philippine EEZ features since departing China on April 15. The vessel operated near Ayungin Shoal (April 20-22), Escoda Shoal (April 22-28), Arellano Reef and Bulig Shoal (April 28 - May 1), Panganiban Reef (May 1-3), and Quirino Atoll and Rozul Reef (May 3-6).

### Philippine Response
PCG Commandant Admiral Ronnie Gil Gavan characterized the activities as clear violations of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), noting the Philippines has not granted consent for any Chinese marine scientific research in these waters. The AFP monitored 62 Chinese naval and coast guard vessels across four key West Philippine Sea features in April 2026.

title: Russia Holds Scaled-Back Victory Day Parade Featuring North Korean Troops for First Time
summary: Russia held its most modest Victory Day parade in history on Red Square, with no military equipment displayed and North Korean soldiers marching for the first time, as a US-brokered three-day ceasefire with Ukraine holds. Putin declared Russian troops fight for a just cause.
contents:
## Russia's Subdued Victory Day Amid Ukraine War and Ceasefire

### Parade Details
Russia conducted its 81st Victory Day military parade on Red Square on May 9, marking the Soviet Union's victory over Nazi Germany. The event featured no tanks or other military equipment rolling over the cobblestones, making it the most scaled-back celebration in Russian Federation history. Soldiers and sailors, some having served in Ukraine, marched and cheered as Putin observed from beside Russian veterans near Lenin's Mausoleum.

### North Korean Participation
For the first time, troops from North Korea participated in the Victory Day parade, representing a tribute to Pyongyang's contribution of soldiers fighting alongside Russian forces to repel a Ukrainian incursion into Russia's Kursk region. The inclusion of North Korean military personnel underscored the deepening Moscow-Pyongyang defense relationship.

### Putin's Remarks
President Putin praised Russian troops in Ukraine, declaring they face an aggressive force supported by the entire NATO bloc and fight for a just cause. He proclaimed victory has always belonged to Russia and will continue to do so, emphasizing moral strength, courage, and national unity.

### Ceasefire Context
The parade occurred under a US-brokered three-day ceasefire running Saturday through Monday, supported by both the Kremlin and Kyiv. Both sides agreed to exchange 1,000 prisoners. Security remained tight in Moscow with air defense systems deployed, mobile communications restricted, and foreign media accreditation cancelled. At least 15 Russian regions declined to hold their own parades, citing drone threats.

### Broader Significance
The subdued celebration reflected growing war fatigue and economic strain. Jailed Russian nationalist Igor Girkin warned of a gradually deepening crisis, noting Russia's leaders appeared more concerned about losing their positions than about the broader trajectory of the conflict.

title: Satellite Imagery Reveals Philippine Infrastructure Expansion on Thitu and Nanshan Islands in Spratlys
summary: Planet Labs satellite imagery confirms active Philippine construction projects on Thitu Island's runway extension and Nanshan Island's port facility in the disputed Spratly Islands, funded by 1.95 billion pesos ($32 million) in Manila's 2025 budget allocations.
contents:
## Philippines Accelerates Spratly Islands Infrastructure Development

### Thitu Island Runway Extension
Satellite imagery captured by Planet Labs confirms a roughly 200-meter runway extension under construction at Thitu (Pag-asa) Island, the only Philippine-administered Spratly feature with a permanent civilian population. The expansion will lengthen the existing 1.3-kilometer runway to 1.5 kilometers, sufficient to accommodate F-16 variants and light combat aircraft. Manila allocated 1.65 billion pesos ($27 million) for this project in its 2025 budget.

### Construction Progress
April 2 imagery shows a barge positioned on the runway's western section, delivering construction materials. The extension occupies an area that previously remained submerged, measuring approximately 200 meters in length and 100 meters in width. Construction commenced shortly after budget approval and continues as of May 2026.

### Nanshan Island Port Development
A separate 300 million peso ($5 million) sheltered port project at Nanshan (Lawak) Island remains in early stages. Satellite imagery from October 2025 through April 2026 documents ongoing work to replace the current port, which has a narrow entry channel too shallow for larger vessels.

### Strategic Assessment
Harrison Pretat of the Asia Maritime Transparency Initiative identified tensions at Second Thomas Shoal as the primary driver for these upgrades, noting they enhance logistics capabilities and resilience of Philippine outposts. Analysts suggest the expanded runway could accommodate US military aircraft if cooperation deepens, potentially shifting strategic pressure on China in the contested waters.

title: Nearly 1,000 Vessels and 20,000 Seafarers Remain Stranded in Persian Gulf as Hormuz Crisis Continues
summary: The International Maritime Organization reports nearly 1,000 vessels carrying approximately 20,000 seafarers remain trapped in the Persian Gulf due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, with the 32nd maritime attack since the war began injuring eight crew members aboard CMA CGM San Antonio.
contents:
## Humanitarian Maritime Crisis Deepens in Persian Gulf

### Stranded Fleet
Approximately 20,000 seafarers aboard nearly 1,000 vessels remain trapped inside the Persian Gulf as the Strait of Hormuz standstill continues. Maritime risk consultancy Marisks CEO Dimitris Maniatis emphasized that mariners caught in the middle represent civilians managing global trade, not combatants, and should not face such conditions.

### Latest Attack Toll
The IMO confirmed eight crew members of CMA CGM San Antonio sustained wounds when the container ship came under attack while attempting a night transit near Oman. The incident, which marks the 32nd maritime attack since the US-Iran conflict commenced on February 28, involved an Iranian projectile striking the Maltese-flagged vessel. The injured Filipino crew members received evacuation for medical treatment.

### Shipping Industry Impact
Lloyd's List Editor-in-Chief Richard Meade reported a 40 percent decrease in strait transits from April 13-20, followed by further collapse to near-zero commercial traffic. The shipping industry received no prior consultation before Trump's Project Freedom announcement and faced confusion as the operation launched and suspended within 48 hours.

### Insurance and Commercial Fallout
Shipping association BIMCO warned that transits without Iranian coordination entail significant risk. Multiple shipowners told Bloomberg they would not contact Iran's new transit authority, wary of providing precise vessel movement details. War risk insurance premiums have escalated dramatically, adding to the economic burden on global trade.

title: South Korea Resumes Heavy Capital Gains Tax on Multi-Homeowners, Reviews Rental Landlord Benefits
summary: South Korea's temporary suspension of capital gains tax surcharges for multi-property owners expired on May 9, reimposing up to 82.5% tax rates. Finance Minister Koo Yun-cheol announced a review of perpetual tax exemptions for purchase-for-lease apartment operators, potentially releasing 25,000 Seoul units to market.
contents:
## South Korea Real Estate Tax Policy Enters New Phase

### Tax Resumption
The four-month suspension of heavy capital gains tax surcharges for owners of multiple properties ended on May 9, 2026. Starting May 10, sellers of additional properties face up to 82.5% taxation (including local income tax) on capital gains. The government initially announced the suspension end on January 23 to encourage multi-homeowners to divest excess properties.

### Market Response
Deputy Prime Minister Koo Yun-cheol stated at an emergency economic headquarters meeting that the real estate market now undergoes reorganization centered on actual residents, moving away from past speculative overheating patterns. Among Seoul apartment listings in March, 2,087 units originated from multi-homeowners, up 32% year-over-year, while homeless first-time buyers comprised 73% of purchasers.

### Rental Landlord Review
The government signaled a review of capital gains tax benefits for purchase-for-lease apartment operators, characterizing their permanent exemption from heavy taxation as excessive from a tax equity perspective. If included in the second-half tax overhaul, approximately 25,000 Seoul apartment units with expired mandatory lease periods could enter the market.

### Economic Context
Koo noted South Korea's March current account surplus reached a record $37.3 billion and exports exceeded $80 billion for the second consecutive month, demonstrating solid economic fundamentals despite Middle East war pressures. The government extended petroleum price caps for a fifth time, freezing gasoline at 1,934 won per liter through May 22.

title: China Confirms Attack on Chinese-Owned Oil Tanker Near Strait of Hormuz, Expresses Deep Concern
summary: China's Foreign Ministry confirmed an oil products tanker carrying Chinese crew sustained an attack near the Strait of Hormuz, with no reported crew casualties. The vessel, believed to have carried a CHINA OWNER AND CREW marking, reportedly suffered a deck fire near the UAE coast.
contents:
## China Acknowledges Tanker Attack Amid Hormuz Maritime Crisis

### Official Confirmation
Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian confirmed during a regular press conference on May 8 that an oil products tanker carrying Chinese crew sustained an attack near the Strait of Hormuz. The spokesperson expressed deep concern about vessels affected by the ongoing Middle East conflict while noting no crew casualties occurred.

### Vessel Identification
Chinese media outlet Caixin reported a Chinese-owned tanker marked with prominent signage reading CHINA OWNER AND CREW came under attack on Monday, May 5. Maritime security sources identified the damaged vessel as the Marshall Islands-flagged oil products and chemical tanker JV Innovation, which reported a deck fire to nearby ships. The incident occurred off the UAE coast near Mina Saqr.

### Diplomatic Dimensions
The attack preceded a meeting between China's Foreign Minister Wang Yi and Iranian counterpart Abbas Araghchi in Beijing on Wednesday, where they discussed reopening the strait. China has maintained its position as a major buyer of Iranian oil since the conflict began, with March imports from Iran largely unaffected.

### Broader Context
The confirmation comes as the US-Iran conflict has stranded hundreds of vessels and approximately 20,000 seafarers inside the Gulf. Traffic through the strait has suffered severe disruption from renewed attacks this week, with the US and Iran exchanging fire again on Thursday while Washington awaited Tehran's response to a proposed ceasefire extension.

title: China Emphasizes Taiwan as Core Priority Ahead of Trump-Xi Summit, Wang Yi Meets US Senators
summary: China's Foreign Ministry declared the Taiwan question constitutes the bedrock of China-US relations ahead of the May 14-15 Trump-Xi summit, while Foreign Minister Wang Yi told visiting US senators led by Steve Daines that bilateral ties have maintained overall stability despite disruptions.
contents:
## China Intensifies Taiwan Messaging Before Presidential Summit

### Diplomatic Signaling
Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian declared on May 8 that the Taiwan question represents the core of China's core interests and the bedrock of the political foundation of China-US relations. The statement demands US adherence to the one-China principle and the three joint communiques as prerequisites for a steady, sound, and sustainable relationship. Defense expert Arthur Zhin-Sheng Wang of Taiwan's Central Police University characterized such strong language so close to a summit as rare for Beijing.

### Congressional Engagement
Foreign Minister Wang Yi hosted a bipartisan US congressional delegation led by Senator Steve Daines, crediting Presidents Xi Jinping and Trump for steering bilateral relations at critical moments. Daines emphasized the desire to de-escalate rather than decouple, seeking stability and mutual respect. He hinted at potential Boeing aircraft purchases as a summit deliverable and recognized China's Middle East engagement, specifically Wang's meeting with Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi.

### Summit Preparations
The May 14-15 Trump visit to China follows weeks of heightened diplomatic activity. China and the US remain in communication on summit logistics. Washington has pressed Beijing to leverage its influence with Iran regarding the Strait of Hormuz reopening. Wang Yi previously told Secretary Rubio that the Taiwan question constitutes the biggest risk in bilateral relations.

### Military Pressure
Beijing continues near-daily deployment of warplanes and naval vessels around Taiwan, maintaining military pressure while simultaneously pursuing diplomatic engagement with Washington. The language explicitly linking US diplomatic actions on Taiwan to the broader relationship represents an escalation in rhetorical framing.

title: Japan SDF Completes Largest-Ever Balikatan Exercise Participation with 1,400 Personnel and Historic Missile Firing
summary: Japan's Self-Defense Forces completed their largest-ever participation in the US-Philippines Balikatan exercise with approximately 1,400 personnel, including the first overseas firing of Type 88 surface-to-ship guided missiles from Paoay, northern Philippines.
contents:
## Japan's Historic Military Exercise Milestone in the Philippines

### Unprecedented Scale
The Japan Self-Defense Forces deployed approximately 1,400 personnel to the 2026 Balikatan exercise, nearly ten times the 150 sent in 2025. The massive increase became feasible through the Japan-Philippines Reciprocal Access Agreement (RAA) that took effect in 2025, simplifying visa procedures and weapons transfer protocols for joint training.

### Historic Missile Launch
During live-fire drills at Paoay on the island of Luzon, Ground Self-Defense Force units fired two Type 88 surface-to-ship guided missiles at a target approximately 75 kilometers offshore, marking the first time Japan has launched offensive missiles from foreign soil. Defense Minister Shinjiro Koizumi personally observed the exercise. Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian condemned the firing, citing Japan's history of militarist aggression and exclusively defense-oriented policy.

### Exercise Context
The Balikatan 2026 exercise ran from April 20 through May 8, involving more than 17,000 troops from seven nations including the US, Philippines, Japan, Australia, France, Canada, and New Zealand. The exercises featured amphibious operations, missile defense drills, and counter-landing live fire demonstrations conducted near the strategically significant Bashi Channel between Taiwan and the Philippines.

### Strategic Implications
Senior Japanese Defense Ministry officials characterized the exercises as effective in checking China's military assertiveness. Former GSDF Chief of Staff Yoshifumi Hibako emphasized that mutual understanding of equipment and command structures can only emerge through joint exercises. Japan simultaneously agreed with the Philippines to establish a working group on transferring Abukuma-class destroyer escorts, potentially Japan's first lethal weapons export under revised transfer principles.

title: NATO Naval Chiefs Gather at Northwood to Shape Future of Standing Naval Forces
summary: NATO Allied Maritime Command hosted its annual Maritime Operational Commanders' Conference at Northwood headquarters, focusing on the future structure of standing naval forces and the integration of uncrewed maritime systems into alliance operations.
contents:
## NATO Maritime Operational Commanders' Conference 2026

### Conference Overview
NATO's Allied Maritime Command (MARCOM) convened its annual Maritime Operational Commanders' Conference (MOCC) at its Northwood, UK headquarters during the week of May 5-9, 2026. The gathering brought together navy chiefs, fleet commanders, operational directors, and senior leaders from across the alliance.

### Key Discussion Areas
Vice Admiral Robert Pedre, Commander of Allied Maritime Command, emphasized that the alliance now operates in consequential times marked by great power competition and rapid technological change. He underscored that maritime power remains foundational to NATO's collective defence posture.

### Standing Naval Force Modernization
The conference centered on the future of NATO's four standing naval forces (SNFs), examining how to maintain occupancy levels, potentially add a third SNMG for the Arctic/High North, and integrate maritime uncrewed systems (MUS) into fleet operations.

### Enhanced Vigilance Activities
Discussions covered ongoing maritime enhanced vigilance activities including Neptune Strike in the Mediterranean, Baltic Sentry protecting critical undersea infrastructure, and Arctic Sentry establishing deterrence around the GIUK Gap.

### MARCOM Evolution
Participants addressed MARCOM's evolving role in warfighting command, maintaining high operational readiness, and preparing for short-notice contingencies across NATO's entire area of responsibility.

title: F-22 Raptor Stealth Fighters Deploy to Kadena Air Base for Indo-Pacific Security Rotation
summary: The U.S. Air Force deployed F-22 Raptors from the 90th Fighter Squadron and 27th Fighter Squadron to Kadena Air Base, Japan, maintaining continuous fifth-generation fighter presence across the Indo-Pacific theater.
contents:
## F-22 Raptor Deployment to Kadena Air Base

### Deployment Details
F-22 Raptors from Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson, Alaska (90th Fighter Squadron) and Joint Base Langley-Eustis, Virginia (27th Fighter Squadron) arrived at Kadena Air Base in Okinawa, Japan. The rotation supports the Department of Defense's ongoing effort to modernize its Indo-Pacific posture through continuous fifth-generation fighter presence.

### Strategic Significance
The deployment reinforces the "Keystone of the Pacific" with the world's premier air superiority fighter. The F-22's stealth technology, advanced sensors, and extreme maneuverability provide total control of the air domain, enabling the U.S. Air Force to penetrate sophisticated defense networks.

### Integration Training
During their stay, F-22 crews and maintainers will participate in rigorous integration exercises with joint and allied partners, refining advanced aerial tactics and strengthening interoperability between U.S. military branches and international allies including the Japan Air Self-Defense Force.

### Regional Context
The rotation comes amid heightened focus on readiness and the ability to respond to real-world contingencies in contested environments, particularly following recent large-scale Balikatan exercises in the Philippines involving over 17,000 troops from seven nations.

title: US Strikes Two Iranian Oil Tankers Amid Fragile Ceasefire in Strait of Hormuz
summary: U.S. forces disabled two Iranian-flagged tankers attempting to breach the American blockade of Iran's ports, while Bahrain arrested 41 individuals linked to Iran's Revolutionary Guard, escalating tensions despite an ongoing ceasefire.
contents:
## US-Iran Maritime Confrontation in the Strait of Hormuz

### Tanker Strikes
An F/A-18 Super Hornet used precision munitions against two Iranian-flagged tankers in the Gulf of Oman on May 9, 2026, preventing them from reaching Iranian ports. U.S. Central Command confirmed the strikes targeted the vessels' smokestacks to disable them without sinking.

### Overnight Clashes
Hours earlier, the U.S. military thwarted attacks on three Navy ships in the Strait of Hormuz and struck Iranian military facilities in response. No American vessels sustained damage during the exchange.

### Bahrain Arrests
Bahrain's Ministry of Interior announced the arrest of 41 individuals allegedly affiliated with Iran's Revolutionary Guard. Investigations continue, though rights groups have raised concerns about the kingdom using the conflict to crack down on domestic dissent.

### Diplomatic Efforts
Secretary of State Marco Rubio expressed hope for "a serious offer" from Tehran, while Washington awaits Iran's response to its latest proposal for extending the ceasefire and reopening the strait. Pakistan's Prime Minister confirmed ongoing day-and-night contacts between the U.S. and Iran.

### Oil Slick Crisis
Satellite imagery revealed an oil slick covering approximately 71 square kilometers spreading from Iran's Kharg Island, its main crude export terminal. Maritime intelligence firm Windward AI estimated roughly 80,000 barrels had spilled since the slick appeared on Tuesday.

title: Satellite Imagery Reveals Expanding Oil Slick from Iran's Kharg Island Terminal
summary: Geospatial analysis of satellite imagery detected a 71-square-kilometer oil slick spreading from Iran's Kharg Island crude export terminal in the Persian Gulf, with potential to reach UAE, Qatar, or Saudi Arabian coastlines.
contents:
## Kharg Island Oil Slick Geospatial Analysis

### Detection and Extent
Satellite imagery analysis revealed an expanding oil slick emanating from the western side of Kharg Island, Iran's main crude oil export terminal. The slick covered approximately 71 square kilometers (27 square miles) as of May 9, 2026, with imagery showing continued leakage from the terminal.

### Maritime Intelligence Assessment
Ami Daniel, CEO of maritime intelligence firm Windward AI, estimated that approximately 80,000 oil barrels had spilled since the slick first appeared in satellite imagery on Tuesday. The cause remains undetermined — potentially a malfunction, an airstrike, or another factor.

### Environmental Trajectory
The spill appeared to spread southwest, potentially reaching the shores of the UAE, Qatar, or Saudi Arabia within two weeks. Greenpeace Germany's Nina Noelle indicated that preliminary assessment showed the spill beginning to disperse, making landfall unlikely under prevailing conditions, though sensitive marine habitats remained at risk.

### Strategic Context
Kharg Island handles the majority of Iran's crude oil exports. The spill compounds the economic and environmental toll of the ongoing conflict in the Persian Gulf, where commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has dropped by over 90 percent since late February 2026.

title: Iran Formalizes Strait of Hormuz Control with Persian Gulf Straits Authority Toll System
summary: Iran established the Persian Gulf Strait Authority requiring international ship captains to pay a toll when transiting the Strait of Hormuz, prompting sharp U.S. criticism and complicating hundreds of commercial vessels bottled up in the Persian Gulf.
contents:
## Iran Establishes Persian Gulf Straits Authority

### RF Signal and Vessel Tracking Implications
Lloyd's List Intelligence reported that Iran created the Persian Gulf Straits Authority, a government agency that vets and taxes ships seeking passage through the Strait of Hormuz. This development carries major implications for maritime domain awareness and RF-based vessel tracking operations.

### Commercial Shipping Impact
Hundreds of commercial vessels remain bottled up in the Persian Gulf, unable to reach the open sea. Ship transits through the strait have dropped by over 90 percent since the crisis escalated in late February 2026. The toll system further disrupts an already paralyzed shipping corridor.

### U.S. Response
Secretary of State Marco Rubio condemned the move, calling it "unacceptable" for Iran to control an international waterway. He challenged the international community: "What [action] should the world prepare regarding Iran's attempted control of an international waterway?"

### Global Economic Ramifications
The strait normally handles about a quarter of global seaborne oil trade and significant volumes of liquefied natural gas and fertilizers. The ongoing disruption has driven oil prices above $100 per barrel, triggering energy emergencies in countries as distant as the Philippines and Thailand.

### Maritime Security Monitoring
A Chinese-crewed oil tanker, registered in the Marshall Islands, sustained an attack near the strait. China's Foreign Ministry expressed concern, noting no casualties occurred among the crew. The incident underscores the growing danger to neutral commercial shipping in the contested waterway.

title: Everest Ransomware Group Targets Major Financial Institutions in Coordinated Supply Chain Attack
summary: The Everest ransomware group compromised a shared third-party vendor serving Citizens Financial Group and Frost Bank, exposing approximately 3.65 million customer records including Social Security numbers and financial account details.
contents:
## Everest Ransomware Supply Chain Attack on Financial Institutions

### Breach Discovery
On April 28, 2026, Citizens Financial Group disclosed a data breach to the Massachusetts Attorney General involving a third-party vendor's systems. The Everest ransomware group had listed both Citizens and Frost Bank on its dark-web extortion portal eight days earlier, demanding payment within six days.

### Scale and Scope
Everest claimed approximately 3.4 million Citizens customer records and 250,000 Frost Bank records. The compromised data included names, addresses, Social Security numbers, tax identification numbers, mortgage interest rates, investment data, and other regulated financial information.

### Attack Vector
The compromised vendor handled statement printing for Citizens and tax-document fulfilment for Frost, giving a single supplier custody of regulated financial-services data from two unrelated Tier-1 U.S. banks. Neither bank's own network sustained direct intrusion.

### Legal Fallout
Six proposed class-action lawsuits emerged across both banks within two weeks of the leak-site listing. The legal theory centers on whether GLBA (Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act) places ultimate accountability on the regulated financial institution rather than the vendor, regardless of contractual indemnity.

### Industry Impact
This attack follows a documented pattern of Everest targeting financial infrastructure, including a claimed breach of Fiserv in early May 2026. The group's operational model prioritizes data exfiltration and extortion over encryption, reflecting an evolution in ransomware tactics.

title: US Revises UN Security Council Resolution on Iran as China and Russia Signal Continued Veto
summary: Washington revised its proposed UN resolution demanding Iran halt attacks in the Strait of Hormuz by removing Chapter VII enforcement language, but diplomats confirmed China and Russia still plan to exercise their vetoes.
contents:
## Revised UN Security Council Resolution on Strait of Hormuz

### Draft Revision
Washington shared an updated draft Security Council resolution with members on May 8, 2026, removing a clause invoking Chapter VII of the UN Charter, which authorizes sanctions or military action. The Bahrain-U.S. text builds on the earlier resolution 2817 (2026) adopted in March.

### Persistent Opposition
Despite the revision, diplomats indicated that China and Russia intend to veto the proposal. Russia's UN mission explicitly warned against "pushing through one-sided and confrontational draft resolutions" that could "trigger a new wave of escalation in the Middle East."

### Resolution Content
The draft demands Iran immediately cease all attacks and threats against commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, addresses the placement of mines and illegal tolling, and reaffirms member states' right to defend their vessels. A non-compliance clause references potential future sanctions measures.

### Diplomatic Complications
A Chinese veto carries awkward timing ahead of President Trump's planned visit to China next week, where the Iran conflict will likely dominate the agenda. Secretary of State Rubio previously characterized the vote as a "test of the utility of the United Nations."

### Gulf Coalition Support
The resolution garnered backing from Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE, whose ambassadors participated in a joint press stakeout at UN Headquarters.

title: Dataminr Detects ShinyHunters Breach of Canvas LMS Affecting 275 Million Individuals
summary: Dataminr's AI platform detected the ShinyHunters cybercrime group's breach of Instructure's Canvas learning management system, with the group claiming theft of 3.65 terabytes of data affecting approximately 275 million individuals across 9,000 institutions.
contents:
## ShinyHunters Canvas LMS Breach Detection

### Initial Detection
On May 1, 2026, Dataminr's AI-powered platform detected an alert indicating that Instructure, provider of the Canvas learning management system, had begun investigating a cybersecurity incident. Enhanced intelligence from Dataminr's ReGenAI provided real-time context as the situation evolved.

### Breach Claims
The ShinyHunters cybercrime group claimed responsibility, asserting exfiltration of 3.65 terabytes of data impacting approximately 275 million individuals across roughly 9,000 educational and corporate institutions globally. Among organizations publicly listed on the group's leak site: Harvard, Stanford, Columbia, MIT, and Apple.

### Confirmed Exposure
Instructure confirmed unauthorized access occurred. Exposed data included names, email addresses, student ID numbers, and messages. The company stated no evidence existed that passwords, birth dates, government IDs, or financial records had sustained compromise.

### Containment Actions
Instructure revoked affected credentials, rotated application keys, and deployed patches to remediate the identified vulnerability. End users of integrated tools may experience re-authorization disruptions during the key rotation process.

### Pattern Recognition
This represents Instructure's second confirmed ShinyHunters breach in eight months, following a September 2025 incident involving the company's Salesforce instance. The repeated targeting raises questions about remediation adequacy and vendor security in the education technology sector.

title: UN Security Council Holds Emergency Consultations on UAE Attacks and Strait of Hormuz Crisis
summary: The UN Security Council convened closed-door consultations on missile and drone attacks against the UAE, as the Secretary-General's Personal Envoy continued diplomatic engagement to resolve the Middle East conflict.
contents:
## UN Security Council Emergency Consultations on Gulf Crisis

### Closed-Door Briefing
Under-Secretary-General for Political and Peacebuilding Affairs Rosemary DiCarlo briefed the Security Council in closed consultations on May 6, 2026, regarding missile and drone attacks on the United Arab Emirates. Bahrain requested the session following Iranian strikes on an oil terminal in Fujairah.

### Attack Details
The UAE's Ambassador Mohamed Abushahab reported that Iran launched 12 ballistic missiles, three cruise missiles, and four unmanned aerial vehicles at the UAE. Air defenses intercepted the majority of threats, though three civilians sustained injuries and a fire erupted at the Fujairah oil industry zone.

### Cumulative Toll
Since the conflict began on February 28, the UAE has intercepted over 500 ballistic missiles, nearly 30 cruise missiles, and more than 2,000 UAVs launched from Iran. The International Maritime Organization confirmed 32 incidents and 10 fatalities in the Strait of Hormuz to date.

### Diplomatic Efforts
The Secretary-General's Personal Envoy Jean Arnault continued regional consultations, meeting with Turkey's Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan. The UN reiterated its commitment to supporting all efforts aimed at a comprehensive and durable resolution.

### Draft Resolution Dynamics
Bahrain and the U.S. circulated a new draft Security Council resolution demanding Iran cease attacks in the strait, supported by Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE. China and Russia have signaled opposition, consistent with their April 7 veto of a similar text.
